seo.title: PII Filtering for AI Agents
seo.description: Complete guide to pii filtering for ai agents. Learn detection methods, fixes, and best practices for production AI agents.

content.intro: AI agents routinely process sensitive user data — names, emails, SSNs, credit card numbers — and without proper filtering, this PII can leak into logs, external APIs, vector databases, or model training pipelines. PII filtering for AI agents is the practice of detecting, masking, or redacting personally identifiable information before it crosses trust boundaries. Failing to implement it exposes organizations to GDPR, HIPAA, and CCPA violations, as well as serious reputational damage.

whatIsIt.heading: What Is PII Filtering for AI Agents?
whatIsIt.explanation:
PII filtering for AI agents refers to the systematic interception and sanitization of personally identifiable information at every point where an agent ingests, processes, stores, or transmits data. Unlike traditional software, AI agents often operate autonomously across multiple tools and APIs, meaning a single unfiltered prompt or response can propagate PII across dozens of downstream systems before anyone notices. The challenge is compounded by the fact that PII can appear in unstructured natural language, making simple keyword matching insufficient.

Modern AI agents face a unique threat surface: they receive PII in user messages, retrieve it from RAG pipelines, generate it in responses, and pass it through tool calls to external services. Each of these transitions is a potential leak point. An agent summarizing a customer support ticket might inadvertently include a social security number in a Slack notification, or a retrieval step might surface a document containing medical records that then gets embedded into a prompt sent to a third-party LLM provider.

Effective PII filtering requires a multi-layered approach that combines regex-based pattern matching, NLP-based named entity recognition (NER), and contextual analysis. It must operate on both inputs (user messages, retrieved documents) and outputs (agent responses, tool call arguments, logs). The goal is not just compliance but building user trust — agents that handle sensitive data responsibly are more likely to be adopted in enterprise environments where data governance is non-negotiable.

examples.scenario: A customer service agent receives a message containing a user's full name, date of birth, and SSN for identity verification, then logs the entire conversation payload to a centralized logging service.
examples.consequence: Raw PII is stored in plaintext logs accessible to engineers and third-party log aggregators, creating a GDPR/CCPA violation and a data breach liability.

examples.scenario: A RAG-powered HR agent retrieves employee performance reviews containing salary information and personal addresses, then includes this data verbatim in a response sent to a manager who only asked about team headcount.
examples.consequence: Sensitive employee data is over-disclosed, violating internal data minimization policies and potentially exposing the company to employment law liability.

examples.scenario: An AI coding assistant agent is given a database dump as context to help debug a query. The dump contains real customer email addresses and phone numbers, which the agent then references in its explanation and stores in its conversation history.
examples.consequence: Real PII is embedded in conversation history that may be used for model fine-tuning or sent to a third-party LLM API, violating data processing agreements.

examples.scenario: A financial planning agent collects credit card numbers and bank account details during onboarding, then passes them as plain strings in tool call arguments to a third-party budgeting API.
examples.consequence: PCI-DSS compliance is violated, the third-party API receives unencrypted financial data it was never authorized to process, and the organization faces potential fines and audit failures.

methods.name: Regex Pattern Scanning on Inputs and Outputs
methods.description: Implement regex-based scanners that run on every agent input and output to detect common PII patterns such as SSNs (\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}), credit card numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and passport numbers. This can be integrated as a middleware layer in your agent pipeline. While fast and deterministic, regex alone misses contextual PII like full names or addresses embedded in natural language.

methods.name: NLP Named Entity Recognition (NER) Scanning
methods.description: Use NER models (e.g., spaCy, AWS Comprehend, Microsoft Presidio) to identify entities like PERSON, LOCATION, ORG, DATE, and CREDIT_CARD in free-form text. NER catches PII that regex misses, such as 'John Smith lives at 42 Maple Street.' Running NER on both the prompt sent to the LLM and the response received provides broad coverage across the agent's data flow.

methods.name: Log Auditing and PII Anomaly Detection
methods.description: Continuously scan agent logs, tool call payloads, and stored conversation histories using automated PII detection tools. Set up alerts when PII patterns are detected in log sinks that should not contain them. Tools like Vex, AWS Macie, or custom log parsers can flag anomalies. This is a detective control rather than a preventive one, but it is critical for identifying gaps in upstream filtering.

methods.name: Canary Token Injection Testing
methods.description: Inject synthetic but realistic-looking PII (canary tokens) into test inputs — fake SSNs, generated credit card numbers, fictional names — and trace whether they appear in logs, external API calls, or model responses. If a canary token surfaces in an unexpected location, you have identified a leak path. This method is highly effective for validating that your filtering pipeline is working end-to-end before deploying to production.

strategies.name: Presidio-Based PII Redaction Middleware
strategies.description: Integrate Microsoft Presidio as a middleware layer that intercepts all text entering and leaving your agent. Presidio combines regex and NER to detect and anonymize PII before it reaches the LLM or any downstream tool. Wrap your agent's input/output pipeline with an analyzer and anonymizer to redact or replace PII with safe placeholders.
strategies.code:
from presidio_analyzer import AnalyzerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer import AnonymizerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer.entities import OperatorConfig

analyzer = AnalyzerEngine()
anonymizer = AnonymizerEngine()

def redact_pii(text: str, language: str = "en") -> str:
    """
    Detect and redact PII from text using Microsoft Presidio.
    Returns sanitized text safe for logging and LLM processing.
    """
    results = analyzer.analyze(
        text=text,
        language=language,
        entities=[
            "PERSON", "EMAIL_ADDRESS", "PHONE_NUMBER",
            "CREDIT_CARD", "US_SSN", "LOCATION",
            "DATE_TIME", "IP_ADDRESS", "IBAN_CODE"
        ]
    )
    
    if not results:
        return text
    
    anonymized = anonymizer.anonymize(
        text=text,
        analyzer_results=results,
        operators={
            "PERSON": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "[PERSON]"}),
            "EMAIL_ADDRESS": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "[EMAIL]"}),
            "PHONE_NUMBER": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "[PHONE]"}),
            "CREDIT_CARD": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "[CREDIT_CARD]"}),
            "US_SSN": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "[SSN]"}),
            "LOCATION": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "[LOCATION]"}),
            "DATE_TIME": OperatorConfig("keep"),  # Keep dates unless sensitive context
            "IP_ADDRESS": OperatorConfig("mask", {"chars_to_mask": 8, "masking_char": "*", "from_end": True}),
        }
    )
    
    return anonymized.text


class PIIFilteredAgentWrapper:
    """Wraps an AI agent to filter PII on all inputs and outputs."""
    
    def __init__(self, agent):
        self.agent = agent
    
    def run(self, user_input: str) -> str:
        # Redact PII from user input before sending to agent
        sanitized_input = redact_pii(user_input)
        
        # Run the agent with sanitized input
        raw_response = self.agent.run(sanitized_input)
        
        # Redact any PII the agent may have generated in its response
        sanitized_response = redact_pii(raw_response)
        
        return sanitized_response


# Usage example
if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_text = "My name is John Smith, SSN 123-45-6789, email john@example.com"
    print(redact_pii(test_text))
    # Output: "My name is [PERSON], SSN [SSN], email [EMAIL]"

strategies.name: Tool Call Argument Sanitization
strategies.description: AI agents pass arguments to external tools (APIs, databases, webhooks). Intercept and sanitize these tool call payloads before execution to prevent PII from reaching third-party services. Implement a decorator or hook that recursively scans all string values in tool arguments and applies redaction.

strategies.name: Structured PII Vault with Tokenization
strategies.description: Instead of redacting PII, tokenize it — replace real PII with opaque tokens that can be resolved back to the original value only by authorized services. The agent works with tokens throughout its reasoning and tool calls, and only the vault service ever sees the real data. This is the gold standard for agents that need to reference PII across multiple steps without exposing it.
strategies.code:
import uuid
import hashlib
from typing import Optional
from datetime import datetime, timedelta


class PIIVault:
    """
    In-memory PII vault for tokenization.
    In production, back this with an encrypted database (e.g., HashiCorp Vault).
    """
    
    def __init__(self, token_ttl_hours: int = 24):
        self._store: dict[str, dict] = {}  # token -> {value, type, expires_at}
        self._token_ttl = timedelta(hours=token_ttl_hours)
    
    def tokenize(self, pii_value: str, pii_type: str) -> str:
        """
        Store PII and return an opaque token.
        Returns the same token for the same value (deterministic).
        """
        # Deterministic token based on value hash (so same PII = same token)
        value_hash = hashlib.sha256(pii_value.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
        token = f"pii_{pii_type}_{value_hash}"
        
        self._store[token] = {
            "value": pii_value,
            "type": pii_type,
            "created_at": datetime.utcnow(),
            "expires_at": datetime.utcnow() + self._token_ttl,
        }
        
        return token
    
    def detokenize(self, token: str) -> Optional[str]:
        """
        Resolve a token back to its original PII value.
        Returns None if token is expired or not found.
        """
        if token not in self._store:
            return None
        
        entry = self._store[token]
        if datetime.utcnow() > entry["expires_at"]:
            del self._store[token]
            return None
        
        return entry["value"]
    
    def is_token(self, value: str) -> bool:
        """Check if a string is a PII token."""
        return value.startswith("pii_") and value in self._store


# Global vault instance (use singleton pattern in production)
vault = PIIVault(token_ttl_hours=1)


class TokenizingAgentMiddleware:
    """
    Middleware that tokenizes PII in user inputs before they reach the agent,
    and detokenizes only when needed for authorized operations.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, agent, pii_vault: PIIVault):
        self.agent = agent
        self.vault = pii_vault
    
    def prepare_input(self, user_input: str, extracted_pii: dict) -> tuple[str, dict]:
        """
        Replace PII in user input with tokens.
        extracted_pii: {"email": "user@example.com", "ssn": "123-45-6789"}
        Returns tokenized input and a token map.
        """
        token_map = {}
        tokenized_input = user_input
        
        for pii_type, pii_value in extracted_pii.items():
            token = self.vault.tokenize(pii_value, pii_type)
            token_map[token] = pii_value
            tokenized_input = tokenized_input.replace(pii_value, token)
        
        return tokenized_input, token_map
    
    def run(self, user_input: str, extracted_pii: dict = None) -> str:
        tokenized_input, token_map = self.prepare_input(
            user_input, extracted_pii or {}
        )
        
        # Agent only ever sees tokens, never raw PII
        response = self.agent.run(tokenized_input)
        return response


# Usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    token = vault.tokenize("john.doe@example.com", "email")
    print(f"Token: {token}")
    # Token: pii_email_a3f9c2d1b4e5f678
    
    resolved = vault.detokenize(token)
    print(f"Resolved: {resolved}")
    # Resolved: john.doe@example.com

strategies.name: RAG Pipeline PII Scrubbing Before Indexing
strategies.description: PII often enters agent context through RAG retrieval. Scrub PII from documents before they are chunked and indexed into your vector database. This prevents PII from being retrieved and injected into prompts. Run Presidio or a custom NER pipeline as part of your document ingestion workflow.
strategies.code:
from presidio_analyzer import AnalyzerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer import AnonymizerEngine
from typing import List
import hashlib

analyzer = AnalyzerEngine()
anonymizer = AnonymizerEngine()


def scrub_document(text: str, preserve_structure: bool = True) -> dict:
    """
    Scrub PII from a document before indexing into a vector store.
    Returns scrubbed text and a report of what was found.
    """
    results = analyzer.analyze(
        text=text,
        language="en",
        entities=[
            "PERSON", "EMAIL_ADDRESS", "PHONE_NUMBER", "CREDIT_CARD",
            "US_SSN", "LOCATION", "MEDICAL_LICENSE", "US_PASSPORT",
            "US_BANK_NUMBER", "IP_ADDRESS", "DATE_TIME"
        ],
        score_threshold=0.6  # Only flag high-confidence detections
    )
    
    pii_report = [
        {
            "entity_type": r.entity_type,
            "start": r.start,
            "end": r.end,
            "score": round(r.score, 3),
            "text_hash": hashlib.sha256(
                text[r.start:r.end].encode()
            ).hexdigest()[:8]  # Hash for audit, not storing raw PII
        }
        for r in results
    ]
    
    scrubbed_text = anonymizer.anonymize(
        text=text,
        analyzer_results=results
    ).text if results else text
    
    return {
        "scrubbed_text": scrubbed_text,
        "pii_detected": len(results) > 0,
        "pii_count": len(results),
        "pii_report": pii_report,
        "original_length": len(text),
        "scrubbed_length": len(scrubbed_text)
    }


def scrub_document_batch(documents: List[str]) -> List[dict]:
    """Process a batch of documents for PII scrubbing before vector indexing."""
    results = []
    flagged_count = 0
    
    for i, doc in enumerate(documents):
        result = scrub_document(doc)
        result["doc_index"] = i
        results.append(result)
        
        if result["pii_detected"]:
            flagged_count += 1
            print(f"[WARNING] Document {i} contained {result['pii_count']} PII entities — scrubbed before indexing.")
    
    print(f"\nBatch complete: {flagged_count}/{len(documents)} documents had PII scrubbed.")
    return results


# Integration with LangChain vector store ingestion
def load_and_scrub_for_vectorstore(raw_docs: List[str]) -> List[str]:
    """
    Drop-in replacement for raw document loading.
    Returns PII-scrubbed documents ready for embedding and indexing.
    """
    scrub_results = scrub_document_batch(raw_docs)
    return [r["scrubbed_text"] for r in scrub_results]


# Example
if __name__ == "__main__":
    docs = [
        "Employee John Doe (john.doe@company.com) earns $120,000 annually.",
        "The quarterly report shows 15% growth in APAC markets.",
        "Patient SSN: 987-65-4321, DOB: 01/15/1980, Dr. Smith prescribing."
    ]
    
    clean_docs = load_and_scrub_for_vectorstore(docs)
    for doc in clean_docs:
        print(doc)

tips.title: Filter at Every Trust Boundary, Not Just the Edge
tips.description: Apply PII filtering at every point where data crosses a trust boundary: user input → agent, agent → LLM API, agent → tool call, agent → log sink, agent → vector store. A single filter at the entry point is insufficient because PII can be generated or retrieved mid-pipeline. Treat each transition as a potential leak and apply defense-in-depth.

tips.title: Use Confidence Thresholds and Human Review for High-Stakes Data
tips.description: NER models produce confidence scores for each detected entity. Set appropriate thresholds (e.g., 0.7+) to balance false positives against missed detections. For high-stakes domains like healthcare or finance, route low-confidence detections to a human review queue rather than auto-redacting, which could corrupt critical data. Log all redaction decisions for audit purposes.

tips.title: Never Log Raw PII — Log Tokens or Hashes Instead
tips.description: Structured logging is essential for debugging agent behavior, but logs are a primary PII leak vector. Replace raw PII in logs with tokens, hashes, or type labels (e.g., '[EMAIL]' or a SHA-256 hash of the email). This preserves enough information for debugging and correlation without storing sensitive data. Ensure your logging framework applies sanitization before writing to any sink.

tips.title: Implement Data Minimization at the Prompt Level
tips.description: Before constructing a prompt, audit what data is actually necessary for the task. If an agent is answering a question about account status, it does not need the user's full SSN — only a masked version or a reference token. Implement prompt templates that explicitly define which fields are included and enforce minimization by design rather than relying solely on post-hoc filtering.

tips.title: Regularly Audit and Red-Team Your PII Filtering Pipeline
tips.description: PII filtering pipelines degrade over time as new data formats, languages, and edge cases emerge. Schedule quarterly red-team exercises where you attempt to bypass your filters using adversarial inputs (e.g., PII split across lines, encoded in base64, or embedded in JSON strings). Use canary token injection in staging environments to continuously validate that no new leak paths have been introduced by agent updates or dependency changes.

cta.heading: Detect and Fix This Automatically with Vex
cta.description: Vex continuously monitors your AI agents for PII leaks across every trust boundary — inputs, outputs, tool calls, and logs — and provides automated redaction, alerting, and compliance reporting so you can ship with confidence.
